title: No False Advertising Found: US Court Rejects Bayer Plea to Block Johnson and Johnson's Erleada Ads
summary: New York: A U.S. judge has rejected Bayer's request for an injunction to block Johnson & Johnson's alleged false advertising that its multibillion-dollar drug cuts the risk of death from ŌĆīprostate...

contentHtml:
New York: A U.S. judge has rejected Bayer's request for an injunction to block Johnson & Johnson's alleged false advertising that its multibillion-dollar drug cuts the risk of death from ŌĆīprostate cancer in ŌüĀhalf.

In a ŌüĀdecision on Friday night, U.S. District Judge Dale Ho in Manhattan said Bayer did not show it was ŌĆŗlikely to succeed on the merits of its claims, which included that Johnson & Johnson's campaign for its ŌĆŗdrug Erleada caused irreparable harm and threatened to erode trust in Bayer's own drug Nubeqa.

Bayer sued Johnson & Johnson on February 23, accusing it of falsely claiming that patients had a "51% ŌĆŗreduction in risk of death" if treated with Erleada ŌüĀinstead of ŌĆīNubeqa, based on testing that adhered to "rigorous" U.S. Food and Drug Administration standards.

The ŌĆŗGerman drugmaker ŌĆŗsaid claims Erleada worked better were unreliable because most Nubeqa patients ŌüĀreceived their drugs off-label. It also said the FDA did not ŌĆŗvet Johnson & Johnson's backward-looking, real-world analysis as a substitute for traditional clinical ŌĆŗtrials.

In a 41-page decision, Ho said Johnson & Johnson's communications accurately represented its study's conclusions, and Bayer did not identify methodological errors that were "so substantial" as to make the New Brunswick, New Jersey-based drugmaker's claims materially false or misleading.

"Based on the current record," the judge wrote, "the methodological choices made by the authors of the study were not errant or out-of-step within ŌĆīthe relevant scientific community."

In response to the decision, Bayer spokeswoman Sue Ann Pentecost said in a statement: "Bayer continues to believe the full body of ŌĆŗevidence supports its ŌĆŗfalse advertising claims and looks ŌüĀforward to the court's determination on the merits of the case."

Johnson & Johnson, in a statement, called the decision "a win for scientific exchange and a strong win for patients.... Real-world evidence helps ŌĆŗclinicians make informed treatment decisions, especially when head-to-head clinical trials data are not available."

About 313,780 men were diagnosed with prostate cancer in the United States in 2025, and 35,770 died from the disease that year, according to the National Cancer Institute.

Nubeqa sales totaled 2.39 billion euros ($2.81 billion) in 2025, while Erleada sales totaled $3.57 billion.
